coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot – Unique reinforcing filler for polymer with special reference to physico-mechanical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Sreenath, P.R.
Singh, Seema
Satyanarayana, M.S.
Das, Prolay
Dinesh Kumar, K.
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the reinforcing efficiency of carbon dots (CDs) in carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene (XNBR) latex at very low concentration. Amine and carboxyl functionalized CDs have been synthesized from citric acid and glycine. The CDs are covalently conjugated to XNBR latex using 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) as coupling agents. The covalent conjugation of CDs with XNBR latex has been confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate have been characterized by ultraviolet (UV) - visible, fluorescence spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer and haze meter. The tensile stress-strain properties of XNBR latex dramatically increases by the addition of CDs to XNBR latex. The maximum tensile stress of 2 phr of CDs loaded XNBR latex is nearly 215% higher than the maximum tensile stress of neat XNBR latex. There is a concomitant decrease in the tan δ peak height and increase in the tan δ peak temperature of XNBR latex with the incorporation of CDs to XNBR latex. In addition, the storage modulus (G′) value of sample containing 2 phr of CDs is 161% higher than the storage modulus value (G′) of neat XNBR latex. The onset of degradation temperature (Ti) value of sample containing 4 phr of CDs is 40 °C higher than the Ti value of neat XNBR latex. On the other hand, the maximum degradation temperature (Tmax) of XNBR latex containing 1 phr of CDs is 11 °C higher than the Tmax value of neat XNBR latex. Morphology of pristine CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate has been analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report which analyzes the effect of CDs on the physico-mechanical properties of elastomer contrary to the other novel fillers of carbon family.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fillers
XNBR
Latex
Physico-mechanical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Covalent conjugation of XNBR latex with CDs and preparation of film
UV-visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer
Digital images of XNBR latex and XNBR-CD conjugate
Morphological studies by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
Synthesis of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate
Covalent conjugation of XNBR with CDs
Optical properties of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate
UV-visible, fluorescence spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay of CD and XNBR-CD conjugate
Digital images of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate
Covalent conjugation analysis of XNBR-CDs by FTIR and XPS
Hydrodynamic radii of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate
Morphological studies by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for synthesis of CDs.
Schematic illustration for covalent conjugation of CDs to XNBR.

(a) UV-visible spectra of CDs, (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, (c) UV-visible spectra of neat XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugates, (d) Fluorescence spectra of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugates and (e) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of CDs and XNBR + 1 phr CDs.
Digital images of neat XNBR and XNBR-CDs conjugate, (a) in solution phase, (b) in solid film phase, (c) neat XNBR film and (d) XNBR + 2 phr CDs film.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) C1s and (c) N1s XPS analysis of neat XNBR latex, CDs and XNBR + 2 phr CDs.
DLS studies showing intensity distribution of (a) neat XNBR latex, (b) XNBR + 0.1 phr CDs, (c) XNBR + 0.5 phr CDs and (d) XNBR + 1 phr CDs.
Tensile stress vs. strain plots of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs.
Tensile modulus of the XNBR/CDs nanocomposite films as a function of volume fraction of CDs (Vf) (the density value of CDs particles is 2.1 g/cm3) [43].

(a) Tan δ vs. temperature curves of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs and (b) Storage modulus (G′) vs. temperature curves of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs.
(a) TGA thermograms of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs and (b) DTG thermograms of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs.
TEM micrograph of (a) pristine CDs, (b) XNBR + 0.5 phr CDs (c) XNBR + 2 phr CDs and (d) XNBR + 4 phr CDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: phr = parts per hundred ml of rubber latex.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tensile properties of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs.
Effect of CDs on the rheological properties of XNBR latex.
Effect of CDs on the thermal stability of XNBR latex.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot – Unique reinforcing filler for polymer with special reference to physico-mechanical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna 801103, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna 801103, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the reinforcing efficiency of carbon dots (CDs) in carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene (XNBR) latex at very low concentration. Amine and carboxyl functionalized CDs have been synthesized from citric acid and glycine. The CDs are covalently conjugated to XNBR latex using 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) as coupling agents. The covalent conjugation of CDs with XNBR latex has been confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate have been characterized by ultraviolet (UV) - visible, fluorescence spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer and haze meter. The tensile stress-strain properties of XNBR latex dramatically increases by the addition of CDs to XNBR latex. The maximum tensile stress of 2 phr of CDs loaded XNBR latex is nearly 215% higher than the maximum tensile stress of neat XNBR latex. There is a concomitant decrease in the tan δ peak height and increase in the tan δ peak temperature of XNBR latex with the incorporation of CDs to XNBR latex. In addition, the storage modulus (G′) value of sample containing 2 phr of CDs is 161% higher than the storage modulus value (G′) of neat XNBR latex. The onset of degradation temperature (Ti) value of sample containing 4 phr of CDs is 40 °C higher than the Ti value of neat XNBR latex. On the other hand, the maximum degradation temperature (Tmax) of XNBR latex containing 1 phr of CDs is 11 °C higher than the Tmax value of neat XNBR latex. Morphology of pristine CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate has been analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report which analyzes the effect of CDs on the physico-mechanical properties of elastomer contrary to the other novel fillers of carbon family.

ce:section.ce:para: The members of the carbon family fullerene, graphene and nanotube have attracted scientist due to their unique properties and numerous applications in various field [1–4]. Carbon dots (CDs), a newcomer in the family of carbon nanomaterials are currently the centre of conversation in nanotechnology. The CDs having size from 1 to 10 nm possess excellent brightness, chemical inertness, low toxicity and high resistance to photobleaching which imparts huge demand to CDs [5,6]. The enormous potential applications of CDs for sensing, bio imaging, lasers, photo catalysis, light emitting diodes and desalination of sea water make these nanoparticles promising alternative to many other nanomaterials [7]. In literature, there are few reports which discuss the procedure for encapsulating hydrophilic CDs in the hydrophobic polymer environment for improved solar cell energy conversion and optoelectronic devices [8–11].

Landi et al. have incorporated CDs and single wall carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) into poly (3-octylthiophene) [12]. Reportedly, the nanocomposite exhibits high electron affinity and high electrical conductivity which can be used for light weight polymeric solar cell applications [12]. Yu et al. have investigated the temperature dependent fluorescence in CDs and revealed that CDs exhibit dual photoluminescence [13]. De et al. have prepared an in situ generated photo-luminescent transparent biocompatible hyperbranched epoxy/CDs nanocomposite [14]. The study revealed that the in situ prepared nanocomposites possessed superior optical and biocompatible properties compared to pristine epoxy as well as the ex situ prepared nanocomposites [14]. Hao et al. have investigated the effect of CDs in polyethylene glycol (PEG) to produce PEG/CDs nanocomposites film for tunable blue-red light emission [15].

The use of CDs for in vitro and in vivo application of oligomeric PEG molecules has been studied by Yang et al. The results suggested that CDs were biocompatible and their performance as fluorescence agents was competitive in PEG molecules [16]. De et al. has fabricated a highly tough thermostable hyperbranched epoxy nanocomposite by the incorporation of CDs reduced copper oxide (Cu2O) nanohybrid which exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity under solar light [17].

Chen et al. have successfully functionalized the carbon dots with polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) via chemical and physical interaction. It was shown that the as-prepared Carbon dot-PMMA film shows good transparency in sun light and the overall thermal stability increased in comparison to the pure PMMA film [18]. Wang et al. have demonstrated the preparation of aminopropylmethylpolysiloxane (AMS) functionalized luminescent carbon dots (AMS-CDs) by one step solvothermal method that could form CDs cross-linked Silicone rubbers (SRs) by self- or co-cross-linking with AMS [19]. Xie et al. have prepared highly daylight photoluminescent and full band UV shielding silane-functionalized graphene quantum dot (SiGQD) nanofluids and their polymerized organic–inorganic hybrid Ormosil gel glasses via a facile and efficient technique [20]. Zhang et al. have demonstrated a facile and versatile approach to fabricate fluorescent polymers using novel polymerizable carbon nanodots. It was shown that this methodology is highly efficient and versatile approach to various fluorescent materials for optical sensing, biomedical imaging, cell labeling and even UV protection for optical devices [21]. Xie et al. have prepared silane prefunctionalized CDs (SiCDs), ormosil nanohybrid gel glass composite (Si-CD-Gel) glasses and macrostructure monoliths (100% SiCD-Gel glasses) which is having good long term optical, thermal and mechanical stabilities [22].

However, till today, there are no reports in literature which discuss the unique method for dispersing CDs in the polymer matrix and understanding its reinforcing efficiency with special reference to polymer-filler interaction. On the other hand reinforcing efficiency of nanoclay, graphene and nanotubes in polymer matrices are well explored [23–25]. For the first time in literature, this work attempts to explore the reinforcing efficiency of CDs in polymer latex. In this work water soluble CDs have been doped and well dispersed in polar hydrophilic carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene rubber (XNBR) latex via covalent conjugation. CDs have been covalently conjugated to XNBR latex using EDC (1-3-dimethylaminopropy l-3-ethylcarbodiimide) /NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide) mediated amide coupling.

The optical characterization of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate was investigated by using ultraviolet (UV) - visible, fluorescence spectroscopy and haze meter. The covalent conjugation of XNBR latex with CDs was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Hydrodynamic radii of XNBR latex with increasing concentration of CDs were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The effect of CDs ratio and extent of CDs dispersion on the mechanical and rheological properties of XNBR latex has been analyzed using universal testing machine (UTM) and modular compact rheometer (MCR). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) has been used to evaluate the thermal stability of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate. Morphology of the pristine CDs and XNBR latex with different concentration of CDs has been studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The superior reinforcing effect and retention of fluorescence of CDs in the XNBR latex is expected to hasten the development of unique polymers having remarkable photo-physical and physico-mechanical properties for multifaceted applications.

Carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene rubber (XNBR) latex, XNB 300 with acid content (COOH) of 6%, acrylonitrile (ACN) content of 28% and density of 1 g/cm3 was supplied by Apcotex Industries Limited, Mumbai, India. Citric acid monohydrate, glycine, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Alfa Aesar, Hyderabad, India. All chemicals were used as received. Nanopure water from millipore was used in all experiments.

The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid as a carbon source and glycine as a nitrogen source. In a typical synthesis of CDs, citric acid (10 mmol) and glycine (10 mmol) were dissolved in 15 mL water and mild heating was done at 120 °C to prevent the carbonization of surface functional group for 12 h, the optimized condition for CDs with high quantum yield (QY). After heating, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally. The resulting solution was diluted to 50 mL and centrifuged at 8000 rpm to remove the large agglomerated particles and the unreacted organic moieties. The light yellow aqueous solution containing CDs was collected and filtered through 0.2 μm filter. The purified CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent conjugation of XNBR latex with CDs and preparation of film
ce:section.ce:para: Primarily, for covalent conjugation of CDs to XNBR, the quantitative estimation of –NH2 group on the surface of CDs was done by using ninhydrin reagent [26] and the -COOH group were determined by titration with 0.05 M NaOH solution. To prevent the interference from –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs during titration with NaOH the –NH2 groups were blocked by formaldehyde [27]. The XNBR was covalently conjugated to CDs using EDC/NHS mediated amide coupling. The carboxyl group of XNBR was activated by adding 0.1 M EDC and 0.2 M NHS to 10 mL of XNBR solution at room temperature. To this activated rubber solution 10 mg (0.1 phr) of CDs were added and stirred for 3 h. Excess of EDC and NHS were removed by dialysis (MWCO- 1KDa). Similarly, CDs were conjugated to XNBR latex in different ratios as shown in Table 1 . The solution of XNBR-CDs was casted in a petri dish for film preparation. The average thickness of the films was about 0.5 ± 0.05 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: Absorbance of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate was measured using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Absorbance measurements were recorded over wavelength range of 200–800 nm at room temperature. Steady-state fluorescence spectra of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate were collected on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan) over wavelength range of 340–670 nm and slit width 3/3. Absorbance and fluorescence measurements were performed using quartz cuvette having 10 mm path length and water as a solvent. Time resolved fluorescence decays of CD and XNBR-CDs conjugate were analysed by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments LifeSpec-II, UK) working on time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) principle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digital images of XNBR latex and XNBR-CD conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The change in appearance of XNBR latex after covalent conjugation with CDs was captured by using Canon D600 digital camera. The images of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate were captured in solution state and in air dried solid film state under UV irradiation. The optical clarity under visible light of neat XNBR and XNBR + 2 phr CDs films (having thickness of 0.5 ± 0.05 mm) were observed and digital images were captured by placing these films on a logo printed on paper.

The haze measurements of neat XNBR and XNBR + 2 phr CDs films were measured by haze meter (Haze-Gard plus Meter 4725, BYK-Gardner, Germany). The haze measurement provides the amount of wide-angle scattering, which is the percentage of light that deviates from the incident beam by more than 2.5° as specified by ASTM D1003.

The characteristic functional group of CDs, XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate were measured by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Shimadzu, Japan). ATR-FTIR spectra were collected in the range of 500–4000 cm−1, 25 scans and 4 cm −1 resolution.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded in an ESCA+, (omicron nanotechnology, Oxford Instrument, Germany) equipped with monochromator Aluminum Source (Al K-alpha radiation hν = 1486.7eV). The instrument was operated at 15 kV and 20 mA. Pristine CDs particles, neat XNBR and XNBR + CDs conjugate films were taken and deposited on Cu tape and degassed for overnight in XPS FEL chamber to minimize the air contamination at sample surface.

Hydrodynamic radii of XNBR latex with increasing concentration of CDs were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Delsa™ Nano particle analyzer (Beckman Coulter, USA). The dilute solutions of latex were sonicated for few minutes before measurement.

Tensile properties of the samples were determined by measuring the maximum tensile stress obtained from the tensile stress-strain plots. Tensile samples were punched out from ASTM Die-C and tested as per ASTM D 412-98 method using a universal testing machine (UTM, Zwick-Roell Z010, Germany). The crosshead speed was set at 25 mm/min at 25 °C using a 10 kN load-cell. Five samples were tested for each system, and the average of the results was reported.

Rheological studies were performed using modular compact rheometer (MCR 302, Anton Paar, Austria) using rectangular specimen having dimensions of average 50 × 10 × 0.5 mm3. The sample specimens were analyzed in torsion geometry in the temperature range of −50 to 100 °C at a controlled heating rate of 2 °C/min at a frequency of 1 Hz. Prior torsion testing, a small axial tensile force (around −0.5 N) is applied to align the sample. From the collected raw data, the software Rheoplus computes the corresponding shear stress and shear strain as well as the shear storage modulus (G′), shear loss modulus (G″) and the loss factor tan δ.

Thermal stability of the samples was determined using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) instrument, TA Instruments, SDT-Q600 (New Castle, DE, USA). The sample (10 ± 2 mg) was placed in an alumina pan and heated in the temperature range of 30–800 °C under nitrogen atmosphere (at a gas flow rate of 100 mL/min) at a heating rate of 20 °C/min. The temperature at which maximum degradation takes place is denoted by Tmax and onset temperature of degradation is denoted by Ti. Rate of degradation and residue is obtained from the weight (%) vs. temperature curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological studies by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of pristine CDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy. Sample was prepared by dispersing 10 μL solution of CDs (0.2 mg/mL) on a carbon grid and air-dried for 30 min for imaging by TEM unit. The bulk morphology of the XNBR-CDs nanocomposite samples were studied using TEM which was performed on thin cryosections of the samples. The cryosections were prepared by ultracryomicrotomy by using Leica Ultracut UCT, Leica Microsystems, (Vienna, Austria) following the procedure reported in literature [28,29]. Cryosection of 300 nm thickness was achieved by using a Glass knife having cutting edge of 6–9°. The sample temperature while performing ultra-cryomicrotomy was kept at −100 °C (which was well below glass transition temperature, Tg, of the XNBR latex film). The cryosections were cast directly over the copper grids of 300 mesh size. The microscopy was carried out using a JEOL-2100 electron microscope (Japan) with a LaB6 filament. The accelerating voltage was 200 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate

ce:section.ce:para: The green and facile hydrothermal heating has been used for synthesis of CDs through carbonization (Fig. 1 ) following the procedure outlined by Dong et al. with minor modifications [30]. The amine and carboxyl functionalised CDs have been prepared with citric acid and glycine as precursors which confer carboxylic and amine functional group respectively. The CDs that has been produced following this method with two functional groups –COOH and –NH2 have high solubility and stability in water. QY of CDs has been calculated using quinine sulphate (QY = 0.55) as a standard and quantum yield of CDs is found to be 42%. The nitrogen doping of CDs through glycine causes more electron and hole recombination through N-surface state which results in strong fluorescence [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent conjugation of XNBR with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Many methods have been applied for incorporation of nanoparticles in polymer matrix to enhance the physical properties of the polymer. One of the standard methods for covalent conjugation of –NH2 and –COOH groups on molecules with high product yield and mild reaction conditions is the EDC and NHS mediated amide coupling. For the conjugation of –COOH group of XNBR in water with –NH2 group of CDs, water soluble cross coupling agents EDC and NHS were employed (Fig. 2 ). The concentration of –NH2 and –COOH on the surface of CDs were found to be 2.4 mmol/g and 9.2 mmol/g respectively. The activation of –COOH functional group of XNBR by EDC and NHS leads to the formation of O-acylurea and NHS-ester respectively. The activated NHS-ester reacts with –NH2 group of CDs and produce XNBR-CDs conjugate via amide bond. The covalent conjugation of CDs with XNBR latex facilitates uniform dispersion of CDs, which in turn significantly enhanced the physico-mechanical properties of the XNBR latex. These results are discussed in detail in the forthcoming sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate
UV-visible, fluorescence spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay of CD and XNBR-CD conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characterization of CDs and XNBR-CDs conjugate has been investigated by UV-visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV-visible spectra of CDs show broad absorption from 200 to 600 nm with one prominent peak at 335 nm that is assigned to n-π∗ transition due to the presence of C=O group [Fig. 3 (a)]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs has been found to be excitation wavelength dependent and shows red shift from 435 to 460 nm when excited from 330 to 390 nm [Fig. 3 (b)]. The maximum emission has been obtained at 445 nm at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

The optical properties of XNBR-CDs conjugates have been analyzed and the maximum intensity was observed at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The fluorescence of neat XNBR, XNBR-CDs (0.1 phr), XNBR-CDs (0.5 phr) and XNBR-CDs (1 phr) were measured keeping the absorbance same for all the samples [Fig. 3 (c)]. While neat XNBR latex shows no fluorescence, their conjugation with CDs induces fluorescence in the hybrid latex. The XNBR-CDs conjugate shows emission at ∼422 nm at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm, indicating blue shift of ∼16 nm as compared to free sate of CDs [Fig. 3 (d)]. The blue shift in fluorescence intensity of CDs on conjugation with XNBR may be assigned to mitigation of surface defects sites [32]. The effect of surface passivation on the fluorescence properties of CDs by XNBR was further analysed by measuring the QYs of CD on conjugation with XNBR using the time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy [Fig. 3 (e)]. The QY of one representative sample (XNBR + 1 phr CDs) has been calculated using equations (1) and (2) [33].
(1)
Q Y c o m p = Q Y s o l n ×
k c o m p
k s o l n
×
τ c o m p
τ s o l n
(2)
k =
(
1 3
(
n 2
+ 2
)
)
2
n
where,
Q Y c o m p
and
Q Y s o l n
( 0.42 )
are quantum yield of CDs in XNBR and in aqueous phase respectively;
k c o m p
and
k s o l n
are dielectric constant of XNBR and water respectively,
k c o m p
k s o l n
= 1.20
;
τ c o m p
( 7.1
n s )
and
τ s o l n
( 5.9
n s )
are fluorescence decay lifetime of CDs in XNBR and in aqueous phase respectively and n is refractive index of medium.
From above equation the QY of CDs in XNBR-CDs composite was found to be 0.61 which indicates increase in QY of CDs by ∼1.45 times on encapsulation in XNBR latex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digital images of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The digital images of XNBR-CDs hybrid conjugate in solution as well as film obtained from this solution have been captured, which shows blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The change in photophysical properties of XNBR latex solution as well as film from nonfluorescent to blue fluorescent indicates incorporation of CDs in the XNBR latex. The fluorescence of solution and film increases in the order XNBR < XNBR-CDs (0.1 phr) < XNBR-CDs (0.5 phr) < XNBR-CDs (1 phr) from right to left [Fig. 4 (a) and 4 (b)]. To have a quantitative estimation of the % transparency of neat XNBR film and XNBR film having 2 phr CDs % haze values have been measured. The % haze value of neat XNBR film is found to be 14 ± 2. On the other hand, % haze value of XNBR film having 2 phr CDs is found to be 13 ± 2. The well dispersed CDs in XNBR latex (at 2 phr) retains the transparency of XNBR film and also significantly improves the physico-mechanical properties. These results are discussed in detail in the forthcoming sections. Similar observations have been found by several researchers in literature [34,35]. For example, it has been shown that the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) nanocomposites retain their optical clarity due to nanoscale dispersion of montmorillonite nanoclays [34]. Bharadwaj et al. have reported that 10% clay loaded crosslinked polyester nanocomposites retain their optical clarity due to the nanoscale dispersion of clay particles in the crosslinked polyester matrix [35]. From Fig. 4 (c) and 4 (d) it is evident that XNBR film containing 2 phr of CDs is as clear as compared to the logo under neat XNBR film which indicates the retention of transparency by the addition of 2 phr of CDs in XNBR latex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent conjugation analysis of XNBR-CDs by FTIR and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: The covalent conjugation of XNBR with CDs has been confirmed by transmittance analysis using ATR-FTIR of XNBR latex on conjugation. The FTIR spectra of neat XNBR latex show the characteristics functional groups i.e. –OH, -CH2, -CN, –C=O group and the spectra of CDs shows the presence of –OH, -NH, -C=O groups. However, the CDs conjugated XNBR latex (XNBR + 2 phr CDs) shows shifting of peak from 1730 cm−1 to 1659 cm−1 for –C=O and at 3295 cm−1 for –NH group which indicates presence of amide bond (-CONH) and confirms the covalent conjugation of CDs with the XNBR latex [Fig. 5 (a)]. The covalent conjugation of XNBR with CD was further analyzed by XPS. The C1s spectrum [Fig. 5(b)] of XNBR, CDs and XNBR-CD conjugate (XNBR + 2 phr CDs) shows characteristic peak at 284.54, 284.77 and 284.8 eV which can be assigned to C-C bond. The peak shifting of 1.49 eV was observed from 287.9 for C=O (COOH) in XNBR to 289.39 eV for C=O (CONH) in XNBR-CDs due to amide bond formation [36]. Moreover, the N1s spectrum [Fig. 5 (c)] of CDs shows a peak at 400.45 eV for primary amine which get shifted to 399.02 for deprotonated amine in XNBR-CD conjugate and it validate the result [37]. This covalent conjugation results in a very good dispersion of CDs in XNBR latex, which concomitantly increases the mechanical, rheological and thermal properties. These results are discussed in detail in the forthcoming sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrodynamic radii of XNBR latex and XNBR-CDs conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of incorporation of nanoparticle i.e. CDs on the hydrodynamic size of XNBR latex has been measured for selected XNBR-CDs conjugates along with neat XNBR latex. The intensity distribution graph of XNBR latex shows increase in hydrodynamic radii in the order XNBR- 210 nm < XNBR-CDs (0.1 phr) −219 nm < XNBR-CDs (0.5 phr) −227 nm < XNBR-CDs (1 phr) −246 nm, on conjugation with CDs (Fig. 6 ). The increase in radii can be attributed to encapsulation of CDs in the polymer chain. At lower concentration of CDs, the heterogeneous dispersion of CDs in large area of XNBR may results in high polydispersity while at higher concentration of CDs homogeneous dispersion leads to low polydispersity and showing sharpest distribution [38].

Tensile stress-strain plots for neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs are compared in Fig. 7 . The tensile strength and tensile modulus at 100% elongation values display consistent increase in magnitude with increase in CDs concentration up to 2 phr. However, tensile strength and tensile modulus at 100% elongation values decreases at 4 phr of CDs concentration. For example, maximum tensile stress value of XNBR latex increases by 20%, 53%, 77%, 215% and 108% for 0.1 phr, 0.5 phr, 1 phr, 2 phr and 4 phr loadings of CDs. On the other hand, modulus at 100% elongation value of XNBR increases by 9%, 18%, 24%, 635% and 343% for 0.1 phr, 0.5 phr, 1 phr, 2 phr and 4 phr loadings of CDs. Elongation at break (%) values gradually increases with increase in concentration of CDs in XNBR latex up to 0.5 phr. However, after 1 phr loading of CDs, elongation at break value decreases. Tensile properties of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs are reported in Table 2 . It is worth mentioning here that well dispersed CDs in XNBR latex results in a strong and ductile material at an optimum filler concentration (2 phr) in XNBR latex. In literature, Xu et al. have observed a significant improvement in the strength and modulus of PVA at low GO concentration (3 wt%) which indicates that the composite film was strong and ductile [39]. Bonderer et al. have reported the formation of high tensile strength and ductile hybrid films when Al2O3 platelets are incorporated to chitosan polymer [40]. The reduction in the mechanical properties at 4 phr concentration of CDs has been attributed to the agglomeration of CDs in XNBR latex. The dispersion of CDs in XNBR latex at various concentrations has been discussed elaborately in the morphological section. Storage modulus (at 25 °C) values from MCR are in line with the tensile stress-strain data. These results are discussed in the forthcoming section.

The reinforcement effect of CDs has been quantitatively estimated by measuring the increasing rates of tensile modulus (dYc/dVf) with different volume fractions (Vf) of CDs in XNBR latex. The tensile modulus values for all the samples are reported in Table 2. The corresponding plot between tensile modulus (dYc/dVf) vs. volume fractions (Vf) is shown in Fig. 8 . It is interesting to note that the plot clearly shows a linear relationship at lower CDs concentrations (up to 1 phr).

In literature, Xu et al. have quantitatively evaluated the reinforcement mechanism of PVA-graphene oxide (GO) nanocomposites by calculating the increasing rates of tensile modulus (dYc/dVf) with different volume fractions (Vf) [39]. It has been shown that the mechanical properties of a ductile polymer composite with two-dimensional nanofillers have linear relationship with the volume fraction (Vf) of fillers in accordance to the simple shear lag model [39]. Cadek el al. have investigated the reinforcement of PVA with six different types of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [41]. It has been shown that straight lines can be fit between tensile modulus vs. volume fraction (Vf) plots of CNTs for each composite film at very low filler concentrations [41]. Similar phenomenon is reported by Fu et al. for epoxy composite with silica where the tensile modulus of epoxy increases linearly with silica loading [42].

Fig. 9 (a) shows the tan δ vs. temperature plot for neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs, studied within the temperature range of −50 °C to +100 °C. It is well known that the tan δ peak will occur at the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the polymer [44]. From Fig. 9 (a), it is seen that glass transition temperature (Tg) of neat XNBR latex is located at −15 °C. It is interesting to observe that Tg of XNBR latex shifts towards higher temperatures with the increase in concentration of CDs up to 2 phr. For example, Tg of XNBR latex with 2 phr of CDs concentration is −12 °C and Tg of XNBR latex with 4 phr of CDs concentration is −13 °C. Tg shift towards higher temperatures is attributed to the good interaction between XNBR latex and CDs. A drop in the tan δ peak height value (tan δmax) of XNBR latex is noticed with the increase in concentration of CDs. The decrease in the height of tan δ peak in XNBR latex by the addition of CDs is due to the reduction of the amount of rubber mobile during the dynamic transition process [28]. In addition, the reduction in the tan δ peak height can also be attributed to the excellent interaction between polymer and filler (filler-polymer interaction parameter) [29]. Tg and tan δmax values of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs are reported in Table 3 . Fig. 9 (b) shows the storage modulus (G′) vs. temperature plots for neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs, studied within the temperature range of −50 °C to +100 °C. It has been found that the storage modulus value (at 25 °C) of XNBR latex increases with increase in CDs concentration up to 2 phr. However, at 4 phr of CDs concentration, storage modulus value (at 25 °C) starts to decrease. For example, storage modulus value (at 25 °C) of XNBR latex increases by 42%, 63%, 133% 161% and 139% for 0.1 phr, 0.5 phr, 1 phr, 2 phr and 4 phr loadings of CDs. The increase in storage modulus value accounts for the reinforcing mechanism of CDs in XNBR latex. Storage modulus values (at 25 °C) of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with different concentrations of CDs are reported in Table 3. The significant improvement in the mechanical and dynamic mechanical properties is attributed to the unique reinforcement effect of CDs in XNBR latex. At higher concentration of CDs (4 phr) the mechanical and dynamic mechanical properties starts to decrease, which could be due to the aggregation of CDs particles in XNBR latex. The dispersion of CDs in XNBR latex at various concentrations has been discussed elaborately in the morphological section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological studies by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used to analyze the morphology of pristine CDs and XNBR latex with different concentration of CDs. TEM micrograph of pristine CDs [Fig. 11 (a)] reveals that the CDs particle appears as dark spherical spots. Inset of Fig. 11 (a) shows the magnified TEM micrograph of pristine CDs. This image further reveals that the average diameters of CDs particle are in the range of 2–4 nm. The diameter of pristine CDs observed from TEM micrograph is in line with the dimensions reported by De et al. [17] Fig. 11 (b) shows the TEM micrograph of XNBR latex with 0.5 phr of CDs. This image further reveals that the CDs particles are dispersed uniformly in spherical form throughout the XNBR latex. From Fig. 11 (b) it is evident that average thickness of the CDs particles is in the range of 50–60 nm. Fig. 11 (c) shows the TEM micrograph of XNBR latex with 2 phr of CDs. This image further reveals that the average thickness of CDs particle is in the range of 60–70 nm. It is interesting to note that spherical CDs particles are very uniformly dispersed throughout the XNBR latex. This ultimately results in enhanced physico-mechanical properties (as discussed in the previous sections). Fig. 11 (d) shows the TEM micrograph of XNBR latex with 4 phr of CDs. The dimension of the CDs particles (shown by circles) are in the range of 100–120 nm. As the filler loading increases, the particles tend to agglomerate. Agglomeration possibly starts to take place after 2 phr, which becomes prominent at 4 phr loading. The agglomerated CDs particles significantly retard the overall properties of the nanocomposites (as discussed in the previous sections). Therefore, it is apparent that the optimum concentration at which CDs significantly improve the overall properties of XNBR latex is 2 phr.

The carboxyl and amine bifunctionalized CDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal heating and the CDs have been covalently conjugated to XNBR latex using EDC/NHS mediated amide coupling. The covalent conjugation of amine groups of CDs to carboxylic group of XNBR latex has been confirmed by characteristic peak for amide bond in FTIR spectra and from the C1s and N1s spectrum of XNBR, CDs and XNBR-CD conjugate in XPS. The fluorescence of CDs has been retained in XNBR latex on conjugation and XNBR-CDs has been found to fluoresce in solution as well as in solid film state. The % haze value of neat XNBR film is found to be 14 ± 2. On the other hand, % haze value of XNBR film having 2 phr CDs is found to be 13 ± 2. The well dispersed CDs in XNBR latex (at 2 phr) retains the transparency of XNBR film.

The hydrodynamic radii of XNBR latex has been found to increase from 210 nm to 246 nm on conjugation with 1 phr of CDs. The tensile properties (maximum tensile stress and modulus) of XNBR latex exhibits consistent increase in magnitude with CDs concentration up to 2 phr. However, tensile properties of XNBR latex decrease at 4 phr of CDs concentration. For example, the maximum tensile stress value of 2 phr and 4 phr concentration of CDs in XNBR latex is approximately 215% and 108% higher than the maximum tensile stress of neat XNBR latex. The reinforcement effect of CDs in XNBR latex has been quantitatively estimated by measuring the increasing rates of tensile modulus (dYc/dVf) with different volume fractions (Vf) of CDs in XNBR latex. The corresponding plot shows a linear relationship at lower CDs concentrations (up to 1 phr).

From rheological studies (tan δ vs. temperature plot), it has been found that the Tg of XNBR latex shifts towards higher temperatures with increase in CDs concentration up to 2 phr. For example, Tg of neat XNBR latex is found to be −15 °C whereas, Tg of XNBR latex with 2 phr concentration of CDs is −12 °C. The storage modulus value (at 25 °C) of XNBR latex containing 2 phr of CDs is approximately 161% higher than the storage modulus value (at 25 °C) of neat XNBR latex. Thermogravimetric analysis indicated that the temperature of onset of degradation (Ti) gradually increases with increase in concentration of CDs in XNBR latex. For example, Ti value of XNBR latex with 4 phr of CDs is 40 °C higher than the Ti value of neat XNBR latex. The reason for the increase in onset of degradation temperature (Ti) has been ascribed to the repositioning of CDs particle at the air-polymer interface which acts as a heat shield thus delaying the heat transfer from the external environment to the XNBR latex.

On the other hand, temperature of maximum degradation (Tmax) value of XNBR latex increases with increase in CDs concentration up to 1 phr beyond which the T max value starts to decrease. For example, Tmax values of neat XNBR latex and XNBR latex with 1 phr and 2 phr of CDs concentration is 458 °C, 469 °C and 460 °C respectively. The increase in the maximum degradation temperature (Tmax) at lower concentration of CDs has been attributed to the result of absorption of the free radicals generated during polymer decomposition by the activated carbon surface. At higher temperatures or higher loadings (above 1 phr CDs) the decomposition of oxidized CDs surface counteracts the stabilizing effect. The rate of degradation at Tmax gradually decreases (reduction in the height of Tmax value) with increase in CDs concentration. The prominent improvement in mechanical, rheological and thermal properties is ascribed to the excellent dispersion of CDs in XNBR latex due to the covalent interactions between the CDs and XNBR latex. TEM micrograph of pristine CDs reveals that the CDs particle appears as dark spherical spots in the range of 2–4 nm. The CDs particle in XNBR latex is found to be uniformly dispersed in spherical form throughout the XNBR latex in the range of 60–70 nm when 2 phr of CDs are incorporated into XNBR latex which significantly increases the physico-mechanical properties. On the other hand, CDs particles tend to agglomerate (100–120 nm) at 4 phr concentration in XNBR latex. This results in reduction of physico-mechanical properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.R.S. and K.D.K. are indebted to Indian Institute of Technology, Patna 801103, India for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work.
